subject: Biology
text: The wide range in the structure of higher plants will never fail to fascinate us. Even though the angiosperms show such a large diversity in external structure or morphology, they are all characterised by the presence of roots, stems, leaves, flowers and fruits. For any successful attempt at classification and at understanding any higher plant, or for that matter any living organism, we need to know standard technical terms and standard definitions. We also need to know about the possible variations in different parts, found as adaptations of the plants to their environment, such as adaptations to various habitats, for protection, climbing, storage, and other specialized functions. If you examine a common weed, you will see that it possesses roots, stems, and leaves. It may also bear flowers and fruits. The underground part of the flowering plant is known as the root system, while the portion located above the ground forms the shoot system.

subject: Biology
text: In the majority of dicotyledonous plants, the direct elongation of the radicle leads to the formation of the primary root which grows inside the soil. It bears lateral roots of several orders that are referred to as secondary, tertiary, and so on. The primary roots and its branches constitute the tap root system, as commonly seen in the mustard plant. In monocotyledonous plants, the primary root is typically short-lived and is replaced by a large number of roots. These roots originate from the base of the stem and constitute the fibrous root system, which is characteristic of plants like wheat. In some plants, such as grass, Monstera, and the banyan tree, roots arise from parts of the plant other than the radicle; these are called adventitious roots. The main functions of the root system include the absorption of water and minerals from the soil, providing a proper anchorage to the plant parts, storing reserve food material, and the synthesis of plant growth regulators.

subject: Biology
text: The root is covered at the apex by a thimble-like structure called the root cap. It protects the tender apex of the root as it makes its way through the soil. A few millimetres above the root cap is the region of meristematic activity. The cells of this region are very small, thin-walled, and contain dense protoplasm; they divide repeatedly. The cells proximal to this region undergo rapid elongation and enlargement and are responsible for the growth of the root in length. This region is called the region of elongation. The cells of the elongation zone gradually differentiate and mature. Hence, this zone, proximal to the region of elongation, is called the region of maturation. From this region, some of the epidermal cells form very fine and delicate, thread-like structures called root hairs. These root hairs are essential for the plant as they absorb water and minerals from the soil.

subject: Biology
text: The stem is the ascending part of the axis bearing branches, leaves, flowers and fruits. It develops from the plumule of the embryo of a germinating seed. The stem bears nodes and internodes. The regions of the stem where leaves are born are called nodes, while internodes are the portions between two nodes. The stem also bears buds, which may be terminal or axillary. A stem is generally green when young and later often becomes woody and dark brown. The main function of the stem is spreading out branches bearing leaves, flowers and fruits. It is responsible for conducting water, minerals, and photosynthates throughout the plant body. Additionally, some stems perform specialized functions such as the storage of food, providing structural support, protection, and assisting in vegetative propagation.

subject: Biology
text: The leaf is a lateral, generally flattened structure borne on the stem. It develops at the node and bears a bud in its axil; the axillary bud later develops into a branch. Leaves originate from shoot apical meristems and are arranged in an acropetal order. They are the most important vegetative organs for photosynthesis. A typical leaf consists of three main parts: leaf base, petiole, and lamina. The leaf is attached to the stem by the leaf base and may bear two lateral small leaf-like structures called stipules. In monocotyledons, the leaf base often expands into a sheath covering the stem. In some leguminous plants, the leaf base may become swollen, which is called the pulvinus. The petiole helps hold the leaf blade to light. The arrangement of veins and the veinlets in the lamina of the leaf is termed as venation. When the veinlets form a network, the venation is termed reticulate, which is common in dicots. When the veins run parallel to each other within a lamina, the venation is termed parallel, characteristic of most monocotyledons.

subject: Biology
text: A leaf is said to be simple when its lamina is entire or when incised, the incisions do not touch the midrib. When the incisions of the lamina reach up to the midrib, breaking it into a number of leaflets, the leaf is called compound. A bud is present in the axil of the petiole in both simple and compound leaves, but not in the axil of leaflets. Compound leaves may be pinnately compound, where leaflets are present on a common axis called the rachis, or palmately compound, where leaflets are attached at a common point at the tip of the petiole. Phyllotaxy is the pattern of arrangement of leaves on the stem or branch. This is usually of three types: alternate, where a single leaf arises at each node; opposite, where a pair of leaves arise at each node and lie opposite to each other; and whorled, where more than two leaves arise at a node and form a circle.

subject: Biology
text: A flower is a modified shoot wherein the shoot apical meristem changes to floral meristem. Internodes do not elongate and the axis gets condensed. The apex produces different kinds of floral appendages laterally at successive nodes instead of leaves. When a shoot tip transforms into a flower, it is always solitary. The arrangement of flowers on the floral axis is termed as inflorescence. Depending on whether the apex gets developed into a flower or continues to grow, two major types of inflorescences are defined: racemose and cymose. In the racemose type of inflorescences, the main axis continues to grow, and the flowers are borne laterally in an acropetal succession. In the cymose type of inflorescence, the main axis terminates in a flower, hence growth is limited. In this type, the flowers are borne in a basipetal order.

subject: Biology
text: The flower is the reproductive unit in the angiosperms, specifically meant for sexual reproduction. A typical flower has four different kinds of whorls arranged successively on the swollen end of the stalk or pedicel, called the thalamus or receptacle. These whorls are the calyx, corolla, androecium, and gynoecium. The calyx and corolla are accessory organs, while the androecium and gynoecium are reproductive organs. In some flowers like lily, the calyx and corolla are not distinct and are termed the perianth. A flower having both androecium and gynoecium is bisexual, while one having only stamens or carpels is unisexual. In symmetry, the flower may be actinomorphic (radial symmetry), zygomorphic (bilateral symmetry), or asymmetric (irregular). Actinomorphic flowers can be divided into two equal halves in any radial plane passing through the centre, whereas zygomorphic flowers can be divided into similar halves only in one particular vertical plane.

subject: Biology
text: Based on the position of the calyx, corolla, and androecium in respect of the ovary on the thalamus, flowers are described as hypogynous, perigynous, and epigynous. In hypogynous flowers, the gynoecium occupies the highest position and the ovary is superior. In perigynous flowers, the gynoecium is in the centre and other parts are on the rim of the thalamus, making the ovary half inferior. In epigynous flowers, the thalamus encloses the ovary completely, and other parts arise above it, making the ovary inferior. The calyx is the outermost whorl consisting of sepals, which protect the flower in the bud stage. The corolla is composed of petals, usually brightly coloured to attract pollinators. Aestivation refers to the mode of arrangement of sepals or petals in a floral bud with respect to other members. Main types include valvate, where members touch at the margin without overlapping; twisted, where one margin overlaps the next; imbricate, where overlapping occurs but not in a specific direction; and vexillary, involving a large standard petal overlapping wings and keels.

subject: Biology
text: The androecium is composed of stamens, representing the male reproductive organ. Each stamen consists of a stalk or filament and an anther. Anthers are usually bilobed, and each lobe contains two pollen-sacs where pollen grains are produced. A sterile stamen is called a staminode. Stamens may be united with petals (epipetalous) or the perianth (epiphyllous). They can be free or united in bundles (monoadelphous, diadelphous, or polyadelphous). The gynoecium is the female reproductive part consisting of one or more carpels. A carpel has three parts: stigma, style, and ovary. The ovary is the enlarged basal part containing ovules attached to a placenta. Carpels may be free (apocarpous) or fused (syncarpous). After fertilisation, ovules develop into seeds and the ovary matures into a fruit. Placentation is the arrangement of ovules within the ovary, categorized as marginal, axile, parietal, free central, or basal depending on the position of the placenta and ovules.

subject: Biology
text: The fruit is a characteristic feature of flowering plants, representing a mature or ripened ovary developed after fertilisation. If a fruit is formed without fertilisation of the ovary, it is called a parthenocarpic fruit. Generally, the fruit consists of a wall called the pericarp and seeds. The pericarp may be dry or fleshy. When the pericarp is thick and fleshy, it is differentiated into the outer epicarp, the middle mesocarp, and the inner endocarp. In fruits like mango and coconut, the fruit is known as a drupe. These develop from monocarpellary superior ovaries and are one-seeded. In a mango, the pericarp is well-differentiated into an outer thin epicarp, a middle fleshy edible mesocarp, and an inner stony hard endocarp. In a coconut, which is also a drupe, the mesocarp is fibrous rather than fleshy.

subject: Biology
text: The ovules, after fertilisation, develop into seeds. A seed is made up of a seed coat and an embryo. The embryo is composed of a radicle, an embryonal axis, and either one cotyledon (as in monocots like wheat and maize) or two cotyledons (as in dicots like gram and pea). In a dicotyledonous seed, the outermost covering is the seed coat, consisting of the outer testa and inner tegmen. The hilum is a scar on the seed coat through which the seed was attached to the fruit, and above it is a small pore called the micropyle. The cotyledons are often fleshy and full of reserve food materials. In some seeds like castor, the endosperm is a food-storing tissue formed as a result of double fertilisation. In others, such as bean and pea, the endosperm is absent in mature seeds, and these are called non-endospermous. In monocotyledonous seeds, the seed coat is usually fused with the fruit wall. The embryo is situated in a groove and consists of one large shield-shaped cotyledon called the scutellum, and a short axis with a plumule and radicle enclosed in sheaths called coleoptile and coleorhiza.

subject: Biology
text: Solanaceae is a large family, commonly known as the 'potato family'. It is widely distributed in tropics, subtropics, and even temperate zones. The plants are mostly herbs, shrubs, and rarely small trees. The stem is herbaceous, rarely woody, aerial, erect, cylindrical, and branched. The leaves are alternate, simple, and rarely pinnately compound with reticulate venation. In terms of floral characters, the inflorescence is often solitary, axillary, or cymose. The flowers are bisexual and actinomorphic. The calyx consists of five united sepals that are persistent. The corolla has five united petals with valvate aestivation. The androecium contains five epipetalous stamens. The gynoecium is bicarpellary and syncarpous with a superior ovary and many ovules on a swollen placenta with axile placentation. The fruits are typically berries or capsules containing many endospermous seeds. Many plants in this family are important sources of food, spices, medicines, and ornamentals.
